concepts.title: Standard form
concepts.conceptBody:
Standard equation of a circle

If the Centre of circle is at origin and radius is a, then the equation of circle is given by

${{\text{x}}^{2}}+{{\text{y}}^{2}}={{\text{a}}^{2}}$

and this is also known as standard equation of circle.

In case the Centre is a point ($h,k$), and not origin then the equation of circle is given by

${{(\text{x}-\text{h})}^{2}}+{{(\text{y}-\text{k})}^{2}}={{\text{a}}^{2}}$

$\text{CP}=\text{radius}=\text{a}$

according to constraint defined by circle

distance $\text{CP}=$ radius $=\text{a}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{(\text{x}-\text{h})}^{2}}+{{(\text{y}-\text{k})}^{2}}={{\text{a}}^{2}}$

This form is known as central form of equation of circle.

General Equation of a Circle

General equation of second degree in $\text{x}$ and $\text{y}$ is

$a{{x}^{2}}+2hxy+b{{y}^{2}}+2gx+2fy+c=0$ ...(i)

The equation of a circle with Centre $\left( \alpha ,\beta \right)$ and radius $\text{r}$ is

$\begin{array}{l} {{(x-\alpha )}^{2}}+{{(y-\beta )}^{2}}={{r}^{2}} \\ {{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}-2\alpha x+2\beta y+\left( {{\alpha }^{2}}+{{\beta }^{2}}-{{r}^{2}} \right)=0  …(ii) \\ \end{array}$

Comparing (i) and (ii)

$\begin{array}{l} \frac{1}{\text{a}}=\frac{1}{\text{ b}}=\frac{0}{\text{ h}}=\frac{\text{g}}{-\alpha }=\frac{\text{f}}{-\beta }=\frac{\text{c}}{\left( {{\alpha }^{2}}+{{\beta }^{2}}-{{\text{r}}^{2}} \right)} \\ \Rightarrow a=b\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{. }\text{ coefficient }\text{ of }{{x}^{2}}=\text{ coefficient }\text{ of }{{y}^{2}} \\ h=0\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., }\text{ coefficient }\text{ of }xy=0 \\ \end{array}$

The general equation of a circle is

${{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}+2gx+2fy+c=0$ …(iii)

where $\text{g},\text{f}$ and $\text{c}$ are constants

To find the Centre and radius.

Equation (i) can be written as

${{(x+g)}^{2}}+{{(y+f)}^{2}}={{\left[ \sqrt{\left( {{g}^{2}}+{{f}^{2}}-c \right)} \right]}^{2}}$

Comparing with the equation of the circle given in (ii), $h=-g,k=-f$

and $a=\sqrt{\left( {{g}^{2}}+{{f}^{2}}-c \right)}$

$\therefore $ Coordinates of the Centre are $\left( -g,-f \right)$ and radius $=\sqrt{\left( {{g}^{2}}+{{f}^{2}}-c \right)}$

Important remarks 

(i) If ${{\text{g}}^{2}}+{{\text{f}}^{2}}-\text{c}>0$, equation (i) represents real circle with Centre $\left( -\text{g},-\text{f} \right)$.

(ii) If ${{\text{g}}^{2}}+{{\text{f}}^{2}}-\text{c}=0$, the equation (i) represents a circle whose Centre is $(-\text{g},-\text{f}$ ) and radius is zero i.e., the circle coincides with the Centre and so it represents a point $\left( -\text{g},-\text{f} \right)$. It is therefore, called a point circle.

(iii) If ${{\text{g}}^{2}}+{{\text{f}}^{2}}-\text{c}<0$, radius of the circle is imaginary. In this case, there are no real points on the circle and so it is called a virtual circle or imaginary circle.

(iv) Dependence of the circle on three unknown parameters: The equation (i) i.e., ${{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}+2gx$ $+2\text{fy}+\text{c}=0$ contains three unknown quantities $\text{g}$, $\text{f}$, c. Hence for determining the equation of a circle, three conditions are required.

Note $:$ In case the equation of circle is given by $a{{x}^{2}}+a{{y}^{2}}+2gx+2fy+c=0$. Divide the equation by a first. i.e. ${{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}+\frac{2g}{a}x+\frac{2f}{a}y+c=0$

then Centre is $\left( \frac{-g}{a},\frac{-f}{a} \right)~\And~ \text{ radius }\left( \sqrt{\frac{{{g}^{2}}}{{{a}^{2}}}+\frac{{{f}^{2}}}{{{a}^{2}}}-\frac{c}{a}} \right)$

Remember that for finding Centre and radius of circle the coefficients of $\text{x}~\And~ \text{y}$ should be equal to 1.

Note: Rule for finding the Centre and radius of a circle: 

(i) Make the coefficients of ${{\text{x}}^{2}}$ and ${{\text{y}}^{2}}$ equal to 1 and right-hand side equal to zero.

(ii) Then co-ordinates of Centre will be $\left( \alpha ,\beta \right)$,

where $\alpha =-\frac{1}{2}$ (coefficient of $\left. \text{x} \right)$ and $\beta =-\frac{1}{2}$ (coefficient of $\left. \text{y} \right)$

(iii) Radius $=\sqrt{{{\alpha }^{2}}+{{\beta }^{2}}-\left( \text{ constant }\text{ term } \right)}$

concepts.title: Paramteric Form
concepts.conceptBody:
Other Forms of Circle 

Diametric Form 

The equation to the circle which is described on the line joining the points $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$ and $\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}},{{\text{y}}_{2}} \right)$ as diameter.

$\left(x-x_1\right)\left(x-x_2\right)+\left(y-y_1\right)\left(y-y_2\right)=0$

Proof:

Let $A$ be the point $\left( {{x}_{1}},{{y}_{1}} \right)$ and $B$ the point $\left( {{x}_{2}},{{y}_{2}} \right)$, and let the coordinates of any point $P$ on the circle be $\text{h}$ and $\text{k}$. (Where $\text{AB}$ is a diameter)

The equation to $\text{AP}$ is

$\text{y}-{{\text{y}}_{1}}=\frac{\text{k}-{{\text{y}}_{1}}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ h}-{{\text{x}}_{1}}}\left( \text{x}-{{\text{x}}_{1}} \right)$ … (1)

and the equation to $\text{BP}$ is

$\text{y}-{{\text{y}}_{2}}=\frac{\text{k}-{{\text{y}}_{2}}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ h}-{{\text{x}}_{2}}}\left( \text{x}-{{\text{x}}_{2}} \right)$ … (2)

But, since APB is a semicircle, the angle APB is a right angle, and hence the straight lines (1) and (2) are at right angles.

Hence, we have

$\frac{\text{k}-{{\text{y}}_{1}}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ h}-{{\text{x}}_{1}}}\cdot \frac{\text{k}-{{\text{y}}_{2}}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ h}-{{\text{x}}_{2}}}=-1$

i.e., $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( h-{{x}_{1}} \right)\left( h-{{x}_{2}} \right)+\left( k-{{y}_{1}} \right)\left( k-{{y}_{2}} \right)=0$

But this is the condition that the point $\left( \text{h},\text{k} \right)$ may lie on the curve whose equation is,

$\left( \text{x}-{{\text{x}}_{1}} \right)\left( \text{x}-{{\text{x}}_{2}} \right)+\left( \text{y}-{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)\left( \text{y}-{{\text{y}}_{2}} \right)=0$

This therefore, is the required equation.

Parametric form: 

If the radius of a circle whose Centre is at $\text{C}\left( 0,0 \right)$ makes an angle $\theta $ with the positive direction of $\text{x}$-axis, then $\theta $ is called the parameter.

Let $\text{CP}=\text{a}$

CM = x, PM = y

x = acos$\theta $, y = asin$\theta $

 

Hence $\left( a\text{cos}\theta ,a\text{sin}\theta \right)$ or ' $\theta $ ' are the parametric co-ordinates of the circle ${{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}={{a}^{2}}$ and $x=a\text{cos}\theta $ and $y=a\text{sin}\theta $ are called ${X}'$ parametric equations of the circle ${{\text{x}}^{2}}+{{\text{y}}^{2}}={{\text{a}}^{2}}$ with parameters a and $\theta .(0 \le \theta < 2\pi )$

Note: 

1.The parametric co-ordinates of any point on the circle

${{(\text{x}-\text{h})}^{2}}+{{(\text{y}-\text{k})}^{2}}={{\text{a}}^{2}}$

are given by $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( \text{h}+\text{acos}\theta ,\text{k}+\text{asin}\theta \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }(0\le \theta <2\pi )$

and parametric equations of the circle

${{(\text{x}-\text{h})}^{2}}+{{(\text{y}-\text{k})}^{2}}={{\text{a}}^{2}}$ are $\text{x}=\text{h}+\text{acos}\theta ,\text{y}=\text{k}+\text{asin}\theta $.

2.Equation of the chord of the circle ${{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}={{a}^{2}}$ joining $\left( a\text{cos}\alpha ,a\text{sin}\alpha \right)$ and $\left( a\text{cos}\beta ,a\text{sin}\beta \right)$ is

$x\text{cos}\left( \frac{\alpha +\beta }{2} \right)+y\text{sin}\left( \frac{\alpha +\beta }{2} \right)=a\text{cos}\left( \frac{\alpha -\beta }{2} \right)$

concepts.title: Equation through 3 points
concepts.conceptBody:
Equation of circle through 3 points 

If three points $\text{A}\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right),\text{B}\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}},{{\text{y}}_{2}} \right)$ and $\text{C}\left( {{\text{x}}_{3}},{{\text{y}}_{3}} \right)$ are non-collinear, an unique circle passes through $\text{A},\text{B}$ and $\text{C}$. To find the equation of this circle, we can proceed in the following way : Let required circle be

${{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}+2gx+2fy+c=0$ … (i)

Since this circle passes through $A\left( {{x}_{1}},{{y}_{1}} \right),B\left( {{x}_{2}},{{y}_{2}} \right)$ and $C\left( {{x}_{3}},{{y}_{3}} \right)$, we have \(x_{1}^{2}+y_{1}^{2}+2\text{g}{{\text{x}}_{1}}+2\text{f}{{\text{y}}_{1}}+\text{c}=0\) … (ii)

$\text{x}_{2}^{2}+\text{y}_{2}^{2}+2\text{g}{{\text{x}}_{2}}+2\text{f}{{\text{y}}_{2}}+\text{c}=0$. … (iii)

$\text{x}_{3}^{2}+\text{y}_{3}^{2}+2\text{g}{{\text{x}}_{3}}+2\text{f}{{\text{y}}_{3}}+\text{c}=0$ … (iv)

Solving these three, we can find $\text{g},\text{f},\text{c}$ and hence the equation of the circle.

Students acquainted with determinants, can put the equation of this circle in determinant form as follows:

Eliminating g, f and c from (i) and (iv), we have

$\left| \begin{matrix} {{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}} & x & y & 1 \\ x_{1}^{2}+y_{1}^{2} & {{x}_{1}} & {{y}_{1}} & 1 \\ x_{2}^{2}+y_{2}^{2} & {{x}_{2}} & {{y}_{2}} & 1 \\ x_{3}^{2}+y_{3}^{2} & {{x}_{3}} & {{y}_{3}} & 1 \\ \end{matrix} \right|=0$ … (v)

(vi) is an equation of the circle passing through the points $\text{A},\text{B}$ and $\text{C}$.

concepts.title: Special forms
concepts.conceptBody:
Equation of a circle in special cases 

(i) When the circle passes through the origin:

 

Let the equation of circle be

${{(x-\alpha )}^{2}}+{{(y-\beta )}^{2}}={{a}^{2}}$

Since it passes through $\text{O}\left( 0,0 \right)$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{(0-\alpha )}^{2}}+{{(0-\beta )}^{2}}={{\text{a}}^{2}}$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{a}^{2}}={{\alpha }^{2}}+{{\beta }^{2}}$

Hence the equation of the circle in this case will be

${{(\text{x}-\alpha )}^{2}}+{{(\text{y}-\beta )}^{2}}={{\alpha }^{2}}+{{\beta }^{2}}$

i.e. $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}-2\alpha x-2\beta x=0$

Note: Thus, if a circle passes through the origin its equation may be taken as

${{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}+2gx+2fy=0$

 

(ii) When the circle touches $\text{x}$-axis

Let the Centre of the circle be $\text{C}\left( \alpha ,\beta \right)$

Since the circle touches the $\text{x}$-axis the radius of the circle $=\text{CL}=\left| \beta \right|$

Hence the equation of the circle is

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~{{(x-\alpha )}^{2}}+{{(y-\beta )}^{2}}=|\beta {{|}^{2}} \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & ~{{(x-\alpha )}^{2}}+{{(y-\beta )}^{2}}={{\beta }^{2}} \\ \end{array}$

Note: If the circle ${{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}+2gx+2fy+c=0$ touches $\text{x}$-axis then ${{\text{g}}^{2}}+{{\text{f}}^{2}}-\text{c}={{\text{f}}^{2}}$. i.e. $\text{c}={{\text{g}}^{2}}$

Hence the equation of a circle touching $\text{x}$-axis may be taken as 

${{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}+2gx+2fy+{{g}^{2}}=0$

(iii) When the circle touches y-axis.

Let the Centre of the circle be $C\left( \alpha ,\beta \right)$

Since the circle touches the $y$-axis;

the radius of the circle $=\text{CM}=\left| \alpha \right|$

Hence the equation of the circle is

\(\begin{aligned} & (x-\alpha)^2+(y-\beta)^2=|\alpha|^2 \\ \text{or } & (x-\alpha)^2+(y-\beta)^2=\alpha^2\end{aligned}\)

Note : The equation of a circle touching \(y\)-axis may be taken as \(x^2+y^2+2 g x+2 f y+f^2=0\)

 

Note: The equation of a circle touching $y$-axis may be taken as ${{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}+2gx+2fy+{{f}^{2}}=0$

(iv) When the circle touches both the axes.

In this case $\left| \alpha \right|=\left| \beta \right|=a$.

Hence the equation of the circle, in the first quadrant, is

${{(x-a)}^{2}}+{{(y-a)}^{2}}={{a}^{2}}$ … (i)

The equation of the circle in the second quadrant is

${{(x+a)}^{2}}+{{(y-a)}^{2}}={{a}^{2}}$ … (ii)

The equation of the circle in the third quadrant is

${{(x+a)}^{2}}+{{(y+a)}^{2}}={{a}^{2}}$ … (iii)

The equation of the circle in the fourth quadrant is 
${{(x-a)}^{2}}+{{(y+a)}^{2}}={{a}^{2}}$ … (iv)

 

Thus, if a circle touches both axes its Centre may be taken as $\left( \pm \text{a},\text{a} \right)$ where $\text{a}$ is positive or negative and radius as $\left| a \right|$.

(v) When the circle passes through the origin and Centre lies on $\text{x}$-axis:

 

Let Centre of circle be $\text{C}\left( \text{a},0 \right)$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ radius $=\text{a}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Equation of circle is ${{(\text{x}-\text{a})}^{2}}+{{(\text{y}-0)}^{2}}={{\text{a}}^{2}}$

or ${{\text{x}}^{2}}+{{\text{y}}^{2}}-2\text{ax}=0$

 

(vi) When the circle passes through the origin and Centre lies on ${{\text{y}}^{-}}$axis:

Let Centre of circle be $\text{C}\left( 0,\text{a} \right)$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ radius $=\text{a}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Equation of circle is ${{(\text{x}-0)}^{2}}+{{(\text{y}-\text{a})}^{2}}={{\text{a}}^{2}}$

or${{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}-2ay=0$

(vii) When the circle cut off intercepts on $\text{x}$-axis and $\text{y}$-axis of lengths $2\text{l}$ and $2\text{k}$ and not passing through origin :

Let Centre be $\left( \alpha ,\beta \right)$

 

$\therefore $ Radius $=\text{CP}=\text{CQ}=\lambda $ (say)

$\begin{array}{l} {{(\text{CP})}^{2}} ={{(\text{CQ})}^{2}}={{\lambda }^{2}} \\ {{\alpha }^{2}}+{{\text{k}}^{2}} ={{\beta }^{2}}+{{\text{l}}^{2}}={{\lambda }^{2}} \\ \end{array}$

$\therefore \alpha =\sqrt{{{\lambda }^{2}}-{{\text{k}}^{2}}}$ [for I quadrant]

and $\beta =\sqrt{{{\lambda }^{2}}-{{1}^{2}}}$

$\therefore $ Equation of circle is ${{\left( \text{x}-\sqrt{{{\lambda }^{2}}-{{\text{k}}^{2}}} \right)}^{2}}+{{\left( \text{y}-\sqrt{{{\lambda }^{2}}-{{\text{l}}^{2}}} \right)}^{2}}={{\lambda }^{2}}$
